required.author: Ingri and Edgar Parin d'Aulaire

required.title: [For Mentor — Read Aloud] D'Aulaires' Book of Norse Myths
required.author: Ingri and Edgar Parin d'Aulaire

required.title: [For Student — Read Aloud or Independent] Biographies of people who demonstrated specific virtues

suggested.title: [For Mentor — Read Aloud] The Children's Homer
suggested.author: Padraic Colum

suggested.title: [For Mentor — Read Aloud] Stories from the Jataka Tales
[For Mentor — Read Aloud] The Book of Virtues
suggested.author: edited by William J. Bennett

suggested.title: [For Student — Read Aloud] Folk tales from multiple traditions

supplementary.author: Robert Coles

supplementary.title: [For Student — Read Aloud] Stories of the Pilgrims
supplementary.author: Margaret Pumphrey or similar early American narratives

competence_markers.description: The student can name at least five virtues and give an example of each from a story or real life
The student can identify when a character in a story acts with virtue or vice, and name which one
The student can explain why a character's choice was right or wrong — not just that it was, but why
The student can compare stories from different cultures that illustrate the same virtue
The student can identify virtues in real people they observe, not only in story characters
The student engages seriously in post-story discussions — offering genuine opinions, not performing expected answers
The student's moral vocabulary is growing — they use words like courage, honesty, justice, compassion accurately and without prompting
The student can identify both the virtue and the vice in a morally complex story — understanding that even heroes fail and even failures contain lessons

narration_prompts.prompt: Tell me about someone from a story who showed courage.
narration_prompts.detail: What did they do? What were they afraid of? Why did they do it anyway?

narration_prompts.prompt: Tell me about a character who made a bad choice.
narration_prompts.detail: What was the choice? What should they have done instead? What happened because of their bad choice?

narration_prompts.prompt: What is honesty? Why is it hard sometimes?
narration_prompts.detail: Can you think of a time when telling the truth was harder than lying? What happened?

narration_prompts.prompt: Pick a virtue — any one. Tell me about a real person you know who shows it.
narration_prompts.detail: What do they do that shows that virtue? How do you know it is real and not just pretend?

narration_prompts.prompt: Tell me a story from two different places that both teach about the same virtue.
narration_prompts.detail: How are the stories different? How are they the same?

narration_prompts.prompt: What is the difference between courage and recklessness?
narration_prompts.detail: Can you give an example of each?

cross_connections.description: The virtues IC1.2 names are the same qualities IC1.1-A1 forms through daily practice. Temperance is what the child practices when they wait. Responsibility is what they practice when they complete their chores. IC1.2 gives names and stories to what IC1.1 builds through action.
Honesty — one of the core virtues in IC1.2 — is the foundation of IC1.3. The child who learns to name honesty as a virtue in stories is preparing for the deeper accountability work of IC1.3.
Responsibility, loyalty, and compassion — virtues encountered in IC1.2 — are the character foundations of duty and service. IC1.5 develops the outward practice of what IC1.2 names.
Many of the stories in IC1.2 come from the civilizations studied in Domain 1. The myths, legends, and historical figures encountered here reappear in the history curriculum with additional context.
The virtue traditions studied in IC1.2 are rooted in the religious and philosophical systems studied in 1.3. The stories connect to larger worldviews.
Narration prompts in IC1.2 develop the same oral and written expression skills that the literacy track builds. Moral reasoning is a form of structured thinking.

content.why_this_matters:
Every group of people that has ever built something lasting — a civilization, a code of law, a family that endured across generations — agreed on certain things about how people should behave. They did not all use the same words. They did not all tell the same stories. But when you look closely at what the Greeks taught their children, what the Lakota taught theirs, what the ancient Chinese taught theirs, what the Hebrews and Christians and Romans and Norse peoples taught theirs — you find the same things showing up again and again.

Courage. Honesty. Self-control. Fairness. Responsibility. Loyalty. Compassion.

These are not rules someone invented last year. They are not opinions. They are what humans discovered — over thousands of years, in dozens of civilizations that never met each other — about what it takes to live well, to hold a community together, and to become someone worth becoming.

This packet is about meeting those ideas through stories. Not lectures. Not definitions on a page. Stories of real people and legendary heroes who showed what courage looks like when it costs you something. What honesty looks like when lying would be easier. What fairness means when you have the power to be unfair.

You will not memorize a list of virtues. You will meet them — in the people and stories that carry them. And over time, as you meet them again and again in different clothes and different languages, you will start to recognize them. That recognition is the beginning of moral understanding.
content.what_this_develops:
1. **Moral vocabulary** — the ability to name virtues and vices accurately: courage, cowardice, honesty, dishonesty, fairness, selfishness, kindness, cruelty
2. **Recognition of virtue in stories** — identifying when a character acts with courage, honesty, or justice, and when they fail to
3. **Moral intuition through narrative** — developing a felt sense of right and wrong through repeated encounter with stories that embody moral truth
4. **Cross-cultural awareness** — the early understanding that people from very different times and places valued similar things
5. **Moral reasoning in simple form** — the ability to explain why a choice was right or wrong, not just that it was
6. **Encounter with heroes and anti-heroes** — learning from those who chose well and from those who chose poorly
content.core_content:
### Part 1: What Is a Virtue?

A virtue is a quality of character that makes a person good — not good at something, but good as a person. It is not something you are born with. It is something you practice until it becomes part of who you are.

Think of it this way: a person who tells the truth once is making a choice. A person who tells the truth every time — even when it is hard, even when no one would know if they lied — is honest. Honesty is the virtue. The single truthful moment is just an action. The virtue is what happens when that action becomes a pattern so deep it is part of who you are.

Every civilization in human history has thought about virtues. The ancient Greeks had a word for it: *arete*, which meant excellence of character. The Romans called it *virtus* — strength of character. The Chinese philosopher Confucius taught about *ren* — humaneness, being fully good to others. Different words, different languages, different times. But they were all pointing at the same thing.

Here are ten virtues that show up everywhere — in Greek stories, in Norse sagas, in Chinese teachings, in African folktales, in the Bible, in Native American traditions, in stories from India and Japan and the Middle East. Not every tradition uses exactly these words, but the ideas appear in all of them:

**Courage** — doing what is right even when you are afraid. Not the absence of fear — the choice to act rightly despite fear.

**Honesty** — telling the truth. Even when it costs you something. Even when a lie would be easier and no one would know.

**Temperance** — self-control. Governing your appetites instead of being governed by them. Not eating everything just because you want to. Not saying everything just because you feel it.

**Justice** — fairness. Giving people what they are owed. Standing up for what is right, even when it would be easier to stay silent.

**Prudence** — wisdom. Thinking before you act. Choosing wisely when the right path is not obvious.

**Fortitude** — endurance. Keeping going when things are hard, without complaining in a way that stops you from acting. Bearing what must be borne.

**Humility** — seeing yourself accurately. Not thinking you are better than you are. Not thinking you are worse than you are. Knowing your strengths without arrogance and your weaknesses without despair.

**Responsibility** — carrying what falls to you. Doing what needs to be done because it is yours to do, not because someone is making you.

**Loyalty** — standing by the people and commitments that deserve your constancy. Not abandoning them when it becomes inconvenient.

**Compassion** — caring about others' suffering and acting to help. Not care that makes people weaker, but care that makes them stronger.

You do not need to memorize this list. You will meet every one of these virtues in the stories you read and hear. And every time you meet one, you will recognize it a little more clearly.

### Part 2: Heroes — People Who Chose the Hard Right Over the Easy Wrong

A hero is not someone who never fails. A hero is someone who, at the moment that mattered, chose what was right even though it cost them something.

**Courage in the Greek Myths: Odysseus and the Cyclops**

When Odysseus and his men were trapped in the cave of the Cyclops — a giant, one-eyed monster who ate men — Odysseus did not collapse into panic. He did not give up. He was afraid. His men were being eaten. But he used his mind, devised a plan, and led his men to escape. That is courage combined with prudence — he was brave, but he was also wise. He did not charge the monster in a rage. He thought, he planned, and then he acted.

But Odysseus also showed a failure of character in the same story. After escaping, he shouted his real name back at the Cyclops in pride — and this brought the wrath of Poseidon down on him and his men, condemning them to ten more years of suffering. His courage was real. His pride was also real. And the pride cost him and everyone who depended on him.

This is why you study both virtues and failures. Even great people fail. What matters is seeing clearly what the virtue was and what the failure was.

**Honesty in the Story of Young George Washington**

There is a famous story — likely invented, but told for centuries because it teaches something true — about young George Washington cutting down a cherry tree. When confronted by his father, he said, "I cannot tell a lie. I did cut it." His father embraced him and said his honesty was worth more than a thousand cherry trees.

The story matters not because it is historically verified, but because it captures a real principle: honesty, even when it invites punishment, is more valuable than comfort purchased by a lie. The child who tells the truth when the truth is costly is building something inside themselves that the child who lies to escape trouble never builds.

**Fortitude in the Norse Myths: The Binding of Fenrir**

The Norse god Tyr knew that the great wolf Fenrir had to be bound or it would destroy the world. But Fenrir would only allow himself to be chained if a god placed their hand in his mouth as a pledge of good faith. Tyr knew what would happen — when Fenrir realized the chain was unbreakable, he would bite down. Tyr put his hand in anyway. He lost it. He did this knowing he would lose it, because it was the right thing to do.

This is fortitude — enduring a real cost, without flinching, because the alternative was worse. Tyr did not pretend it would not hurt. He did not try to find a way around the sacrifice. He accepted the cost and acted.

**Justice in African Tradition: The Story of Mansa Musa's Pilgrimage**

Mansa Musa, the ruler of the Mali Empire in the 14th century, was one of the wealthiest people who ever lived. On his famous pilgrimage to Mecca, he gave away so much gold that he destabilized the economies of the cities he passed through. But more important than his wealth was his reputation for justice. He governed his empire by law, not by whim. He treated subjects fairly. He built institutions of learning. His power was enormous — and he used it for the good of those he governed, not merely for himself. That is justice exercised by someone who had the power to be unjust.

**Compassion in the Jataka Tales: The Monkey King**

In one of the Jataka tales — Buddhist stories about the previous lives of the Buddha — a monkey king discovers that his troop of monkeys is trapped and will be killed by a human king's soldiers. The monkey king stretches his own body across a chasm, creating a bridge so his monkeys can escape over him. His body is broken by the weight. When the human king asks why he sacrificed himself, the monkey king says, "I am their leader. Their well-being is my responsibility."

This is compassion that costs everything. Not compassion as a feeling — compassion as action, action that requires sacrifice.

### Part 3: Anti-Heroes — People Who Failed, and What We Learn From Their Failures

Not every character in a story is a hero. Some are anti-heroes — people who had the chance to do right and failed. Sometimes they failed because of cowardice. Sometimes because of greed. Sometimes because of pride. Studying their failures teaches as much as studying the hero's success.

**The Failure of Achilles: Wrath Without Justice**

Achilles was the greatest warrior of the Greeks. He was nearly invincible in battle. But when Agamemnon dishonored him by taking his war prize, Achilles withdrew from the fighting in a rage. He was not wrong to feel angry — the injustice was real. But his response to the injustice was to let his friends die while he sulked in his tent. His best friend Patroclus went into battle in his place and was killed.

Achilles had courage. He had immense physical fortitude. But he lacked temperance — the ability to control his anger — and he lacked justice in his response. His rage served himself, not his people. The lesson is brutal: courage and strength without self-governance and justice produce destruction, even in the most gifted people.

**The Failure of Loki: Cleverness Without Loyalty**

In Norse mythology, Loki is the trickster. He is the cleverest of the gods — witty, inventive, always able to talk his way out of trouble. But he has no loyalty. He helps the gods when it amuses him and betrays them when that amuses him more. Over time, his betrayals mount. He causes the death of the beloved god Baldur, and eventually the gods bind him in punishment.

Loki teaches that intelligence without loyalty is dangerous. A clever person who cannot be trusted is worse than a foolish person who can. Cleverness is not a virtue. It is a tool. What determines its worth is what it serves.

**The Failure of King Midas: Greed Without Prudence**

King Midas was granted a wish and asked that everything he touched turn to gold. It seemed like wisdom — gold is valuable. But he had not thought it through. His food turned to gold. His drink turned to gold. His daughter, when he embraced her, turned to gold. He had power without wisdom, desire without prudence. He got exactly what he asked for and it destroyed everything he loved.

The story teaches that wanting more is not the same as wanting wisely. Prudence — the virtue of thinking through consequences before acting — would have saved Midas. Its absence ruined him.

### Part 4: Virtue Is the Same Everywhere — and That Matters

One of the most important things you will notice as you hear more stories from more places is this: people everywhere came to the same conclusions about what makes a person good.

The Greeks valued courage, wisdom, justice, and self-control. They called these the four cardinal virtues.

The Norse valued courage above all — but also loyalty, hospitality, and honor. A warrior who broke his oath was worse than a coward.

The Chinese teacher Confucius taught that a good person practices *ren* (kindness and humanity toward others), *yi* (righteousness — doing what is right even when it is costly), and *li* (propriety — the discipline of behaving correctly in your role).

The traditions of many Indigenous peoples — Lakota, Apache, Maori, and others — taught courage, endurance, generosity, and respect for elders. A warrior was not just strong. A warrior was responsible, loyal, and self-governed.

The Christian tradition taught that the greatest virtues are faith, hope, and love — alongside the same four cardinal virtues the Greeks identified.

The Jewish tradition taught justice (*tzedakah*), loving-kindness (*chesed*), and walking humbly before God.

Different words. Different stories. Different times and places. But the same core idea: there is a way to be that is good. There are qualities of character that make a person trustworthy, strong, fair, and worthy of respect. And these qualities are not easy. They cost something. They require effort, sacrifice, and the willingness to do hard things.

The fact that people everywhere — without copying each other, without ever meeting each other — came to these same conclusions should tell you something. These are not arbitrary rules. They are discoveries about what human beings need to be in order to live well together and build something lasting.

### Part 5: Virtue in the People Around You

Stories are powerful, but the most powerful formation happens not through stories but through people. The adults in your life — your parents, your mentors, your family members — demonstrate virtue every day in ways you may not notice because they are not dramatic.

The parent who goes to work when they are tired is demonstrating fortitude.

The adult who admits a mistake and says "I was wrong" is demonstrating honesty and humility.

The neighbor who brings food to a sick family is demonstrating compassion.

The person who keeps a promise even when it becomes inconvenient is demonstrating loyalty and responsibility.

Pay attention. When you see someone do something that costs them something — that would have been easier not to do — ask yourself: which virtue was that? Start noticing. The world is full of ordinary courage, quiet honesty, and steady responsibility. These are not lesser because they are not in a story. They are greater — because they are real.

### Part 6: Virtue and Vice — Recognizing the Opposite

Every virtue has a corresponding vice — its opposite. Learning to name both is part of developing moral clarity.

- Courage — Cowardice (failing to act rightly because of fear)
- Honesty — Dishonesty (lying, deceiving, hiding the truth)
- Temperance — Excess (being controlled by appetites, unable to say no to yourself)
- Justice — Injustice (taking what is not yours, failing to give what is owed)
- Prudence — Recklessness (acting without thinking, ignoring consequences)
- Fortitude — Collapse (giving up when things are hard)
- Humility — Arrogance (thinking you are more than you are) or Self-Pity (thinking you are less)
- Responsibility — Negligence (letting things fall because you will not carry them)
- Loyalty — Betrayal (abandoning those who count on you)
- Compassion — Cruelty (indifference to suffering) or Enabling (care that weakens instead of strengthens)

When you read a story or see something happen in real life, practice identifying not only the virtue but the vice. "He was dishonest there." "She showed courage." "That was unjust." The ability to name what you see — accurately, without flinching — is the beginning of moral reasoning.
content.formative_practices:
### Practice 1: Story and Discussion [Weekly — Minimum]
Read or listen to at least one story per week that involves a character making a moral choice. After the story, discuss with your mentor:
- What did the character choose?
- Was it the right choice? Why or why not?
- What virtue did they show? Or what vice?
- What would you have done?
- What did the choice cost them?

The conversation is the practice. The story is the vehicle.

### Practice 2: Virtue Vocabulary [Ongoing]
As you encounter virtues and vices in stories and in life, name them. Build your vocabulary. When you see courage, call it courage. When you see dishonesty, call it dishonesty. The mentor should reinforce this: "That was an act of compassion. Did you notice?" Over time, the child begins to name virtues without prompting.

### Practice 3: Virtue Spotting in Real Life [Ongoing]
Once a week, identify one act of virtue you observed in a real person — not a character in a story, but someone you actually saw. Describe what they did and which virtue it demonstrated. This trains the habit of noticing character in the real world.

### Practice 4: Drawing or Narrating a Virtue Scene [Biweekly]
After a particularly compelling story, draw a picture of the moment the character made their choice — or narrate the scene in your own words. For younger students, drawing is often the most natural way to process a story. For older Stretching Branches students, narration builds both moral reasoning and communication skill.

### Practice 5: Comparing Stories Across Cultures [Monthly]
Once a month, compare two stories from different cultures that illustrate the same virtue. "The Greek story of Tyr putting his hand in the wolf's mouth and the story of Harriet Tubman going back into the South — how are they the same? Both showed courage. But the courage looked different." This practice builds the cross-cultural awareness that IC1.2 specifically develops.

### Practice 6: Anti-Hero Analysis [As Stories Arise]
When a story includes a character who fails morally, discuss the failure specifically. Not "he was bad" but "he was greedy, and here is what his greed cost." The ability to analyze failure teaches as much as the ability to admire success.

mentor_guide.formation_environment:
### Story Selection Matters

Not all stories teach equally. The stories that form moral understanding share specific qualities:

**The choice costs something.** Stories where the hero does the right thing and it works out easily do not teach virtue. Virtue is only meaningful when it is costly. Choose stories where the right action requires sacrifice, loss, or risk. Odysseus loses his hand. Harriet Tubman risks recapture. The monkey king breaks his own body. The cost is the lesson.

**The characters are complex.** Avoid stories where the hero is flawless and the villain is pure evil. Real moral formation happens when children encounter characters who are brave AND flawed, kind AND mistaken. Achilles is the greatest warrior and also a petulant man whose anger kills his best friend. That complexity is essential — it mirrors the real world where good people fail and difficult people occasionally do the right thing.

**The tradition varies.** IC1.2 is specifically cross-cultural. Do not draw all stories from one tradition. The child should encounter Greek myths, Norse sagas, African folktales, Asian teaching stories, Indigenous narratives, Biblical accounts, and historical biography from multiple civilizations. The diversity is not decorative — it IS the curriculum. The convergence of virtue across cultures is the central insight of IC1.2.

### Discussion, Not Lecture

The post-story conversation is where formation happens. The child who listens to a story passively absorbs some of its moral content. The child who is asked to articulate what they think, explain why, and defend their reasoning internalizes it at a deeper level.

**Your job in the discussion:**
1. Ask questions. Do not deliver answers.
2. Accept the child's genuine moral reasoning, even when it is imperfect. "Because he was scared" is a valid observation from a six-year-old, even if it is incomplete.
3. Push gently for more depth: "He was scared — and what did he do even though he was scared? What do we call that?"
4. Never mock or dismiss a child's moral opinion. If they say something you disagree with — "I think Achilles was right to stop fighting" — explore it: "Tell me more about why you think that. What about the men who died while he sat in his tent?"
5. Introduce virtue vocabulary naturally: "That sounds like what we call courage." Over time, the child begins to use the vocabulary themselves.

### Age Calibration Within Stretching Branches

Stretching Branches spans ages 4 through 9, which is an enormous developmental range. Calibrate accordingly:

**Ages 4-5:** Stories are short and simple. Read picture books and brief folk tales. The discussion is three or four questions, lasting five minutes. Focus on one virtue per story. "Was that brave?" is enough.

**Ages 6-7:** Stories can be longer — chapter book read-alouds, more complex myths. Discussions can last ten minutes. The child should begin identifying virtues without prompting: "I think that was honesty." Introduce the concept that even good characters make mistakes.

**Ages 8-9:** Full-length myth retellings, biographies, and more morally complex stories. Discussions can be substantive — fifteen to twenty minutes. The child should be comparing stories across traditions: "That is like the story from last week, because both of them showed courage but in different ways." Anti-hero analysis becomes productive at this age.
mentor_guide.competence_evaluation:
### 1. Virtue Vocabulary
**Developing well:** The child names virtues accurately and spontaneously. They use words like "courage," "honesty," "justice," "compassion" in conversation — not just in response to your prompts, but when describing things they see or hear. They can define the virtues in their own words.

**Developing slowly:** The child cannot name virtues without prompting. Their moral vocabulary is limited to "good" and "bad" without specificity. They do not use virtue language outside of structured discussion.

**Diagnostic questions:** Are you using virtue vocabulary yourself, consistently, in daily life? The child will mirror the language they hear. Are the stories simple enough for their age? An overwhelmed child will not absorb vocabulary. Are you giving them enough time — this vocabulary develops over months, not weeks.

### 2. Virtue Identification in Stories
**Developing well:** When asked "What virtue did that character show?" the child can answer accurately most of the time. They are beginning to identify virtues without being asked. They notice both virtues and vices: "He was brave, but he was also too proud."

**Developing slowly:** The child cannot connect a character's action to a virtue name. They describe the action but cannot categorize it. "He put his hand in the wolf's mouth" without connecting it to courage.

**Diagnostic questions:** Are you asking the identification question consistently after every story? Repetition builds the connection. Are you modeling the identification yourself first, then asking the child to try? Is the story too complex for the child to follow the moral thread?

### 3. Moral Reasoning — the "Why"
**Developing well:** The child can explain why a choice was right or wrong. Not just "because it was mean" but "because he broke his promise and the people who trusted him got hurt." The reasoning connects action to consequence and principle.

**Developing slowly:** The child can identify right and wrong but cannot explain why. Answers stay at "because it's bad" without further development.

**Diagnostic questions:** Are you pushing gently past the first answer? "It was bad — tell me more about why it was bad. What happened because of that choice?" Moral reasoning develops through practice, and the practice is the conversation.

### 4. Cross-Cultural Recognition
**Developing well:** The child spontaneously notices when a story from one culture echoes a virtue from another. "That's like the story about Tyr — they both were brave even though they knew it would hurt." They are beginning to grasp the pattern: different people, same virtues.

**Developing slowly:** Each story exists in isolation. The child does not connect stories across traditions without heavy prompting.

**Diagnostic questions:** Are you explicitly making the comparisons at first? "Remember the story of ___? This story is about the same thing — courage. But the courage looks different." The child needs modeling before they do this independently.

### 5. Real-World Virtue Spotting
**Developing well:** The child identifies virtues in real people — family members, neighbors, people they observe. "Dad showed patience today when the car broke down." This is the transfer from story to life, and it indicates deep understanding.

**Developing slowly:** Virtues remain story-only concepts. The child does not connect the vocabulary to real behavior they observe.

**Diagnostic questions:** Are you pointing out real-world virtues yourself? "Did you see what Mrs. Johnson did? She brought food to the Smiths because they are sick. What virtue is that?" Model the transfer first.

### 6. Story Engagement Quality
**Developing well:** The child is genuinely engaged during stories and discussions. They ask questions, offer opinions, express disagreement, show emotional responses to characters' choices. They remember stories across weeks and reference them.

**Developing slowly:** Passive listening. No engagement in discussion. Cannot recall stories from previous weeks. Gives performative answers ("He was brave because that's the right answer") rather than genuine responses.

**Diagnostic questions:** Are the stories age-appropriate and engaging? Is the reading environment focused and free from distraction? Are discussions conversational rather than interrogative?
mentor_guide.common_pitfalls:
### Moralizing Instead of Discussing
The single most destructive thing you can do in IC1.2-A is turn every story into a lecture. "And THAT is why you should always tell the truth." The child will tune out. They will associate virtue language with being talked at. And they will learn to give the expected answer rather than to think.

The cure: ask questions. Let the child draw the conclusion. If they draw it imperfectly, ask another question. Resist the urge to deliver the lesson. The lesson is theirs to find.

### Sanitizing the Stories
Myths and legends contain violence, death, betrayal, and loss. These elements are not incidental — they are what make the stories morally meaningful. A courage story without real danger is not about courage. A loyalty story without betrayal's temptation is not about loyalty.

Calibrate to the child's age, but do not strip the stories of their moral weight. A five-year-old does not need graphic details. But they can understand "the hero was hurt" and "the man died because he chose to save his friends." Children are not fragile. They need stories that take them seriously.

### Drawing All Stories From One Tradition
If all your stories come from Greek mythology, or all from the Bible, or all from one cultural tradition, you are undermining the central insight of IC1.2: that virtue is universal. The diversity of sources is the curriculum. Rotate traditions deliberately. Track which cultures you have drawn from and which you have not.

### Treating Virtues as a Checklist
IC1.2 lists ten core virtues. The temptation is to march through them one per week, check them off, and declare the packet complete. This misses the point entirely. Virtues are not topics to cover. They are realities to encounter, recognize, and internalize. Some virtues will come up repeatedly because the stories naturally emphasize them. Others will emerge slowly. Follow the stories, not a checklist.

### Forcing Agreement
A child who says "I think Achilles was right to stop fighting" is engaging in moral reasoning. A child who says what they think you want to hear is not. Protect the child's genuine thinking, even when it is wrong. You can challenge it with questions — "What about the men who died?" — but do not punish independent moral thought by overriding it with your conclusion. The child who reasons badly and is guided toward better reasoning learns more than the child who memorizes the right answer.
mentor_guide.narration_evaluation:
### Prompt 1: A Hero's Courage
**Strong:** Names a specific character and a specific act. Describes what the character was afraid of and why they acted anyway. Identifies the cost. "Tyr put his hand in the wolf's mouth even though he knew the wolf would bite it off, because if he didn't, no one could chain the wolf and it would destroy everything."
**Weak:** "Someone was brave in a story." No specifics, no connection to cost.

### Prompt 2: A Bad Choice
**Strong:** Names the character, the choice, the consequence, and what should have been done differently. "Achilles stopped fighting because he was angry, and his friend Patroclus went to fight instead and got killed. Achilles should have kept fighting even though he was angry."
**Weak:** "A guy was bad." No specifics.

### Prompt 3: Honesty
**Strong:** Can describe a personal or observed experience of honesty being difficult. Articulates why lying was tempting and why honesty mattered. "I broke the vase and I wanted to say the dog did it but I told Mom I did it because lying is worse than getting in trouble."
**Weak:** "You should always tell the truth." Abstract, not personal.

### Prompt 4: Virtue in a Real Person
**Strong:** Names a specific person and a specific behavior. Connects it to a virtue by name. "My grandfather works every day even when he's tired. That's fortitude — he keeps going."
**Weak:** "My mom is nice." No specificity, no virtue name.

### Prompt 5: Cross-Cultural Comparison
**Strong:** Names two stories from different traditions, identifies the shared virtue, and describes how the expression differs. "The Jataka monkey king and Harriet Tubman both showed courage and compassion — they risked themselves for other people. But the monkey king died and Harriet Tubman kept going back again and again."
**Weak:** "Two stories were about being good." No specifics, no analysis.

### Prompt 6: Courage vs. Recklessness
**Strong:** Can distinguish between the two with examples. "Courage is doing something scary because it is right. Recklessness is doing something scary because you want to show off or because you didn't think about what would happen."
**Weak:** Cannot distinguish. "They're both when you do something scary."
